message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 12
Recent Green phase: Added rune: 25 to BASE_PREMIUM.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll perform the Refactor phase following the disciplined process.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md
eslint.config.mjs
example-mapping
metrics.json
node_modules
package.json
pnpm-lock.yaml
prompt.md
run.log
src
tdd-journal.md
tsconfig.json
vitest.config.ts

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: tightened `runScenario` return type from `{ results: unknown[] }` to `{ results: { premium: number }[] }`
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — documents the shape tests exercise; `premium: number` field-name contract is now visible at the signature
6	- Naming established: `runScenario(scenario) -> { results: { premium: number }[] }`; field name `premium` confirmed (per example-mapping contract)
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: extracted magic numbers in `runScenario` to module-level named constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1` and `PROCESSING_FEE = 5`
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — these are global pricing-rule constants that more upcoming tests will reference; naming them centralises and documents intent at every use site. Rule 2 trumps APP (mass +5).
11	- Naming established: `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` (10% surcharge on first-insurance, applied to policy base), `PROCESSING_FEE` (flat 5 G added to every quote), `BASE_PREMIUM` (lookup table by item type); per-step local names `policyBase` (sum of item base premiums) and `firstInsurance` (surcharge amount, not boolean flag)
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: extracted per-step pricing into a private module-level function `priceQuoteStep(step: QuoteStep) -> { premium: number }`; `runScenario` is now just `scenario.steps.map(priceQuoteStep)`
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — names the "price one quote step" concept that was previously anonymous inside `.map(...)`. Upcoming surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty, follow-up) have a clear home and won't bloat `runScenario`. Mass roughly neutral.
16	- Naming established: `priceQuoteStep(step)` is the canonical per-step pricing helper; future surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty discount, follow-up discount) should compose inside this function, not in `runScenario`
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- Change: extracted `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` helpers; named the `0.5` magic number as `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`; renamed local `curseSurcharge` to `itemSurchargeTotal` (now an accumulator for all per-item surcharges, not just curse).
20	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` lookup was duplicated across two reduces; now centralised in `itemBasePremium`. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `itemSurcharges(item)` is the natural extension point for upcoming high-enchantment (per-item) surcharge.
21	- Naming established: `itemBasePremium(item) -> number` (single source of base-premium lookup, will absorb rune-block logic later); `itemSurcharges(item) -> number` (sum of all per-item surcharges — add high-enchantment here next); `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5`; the local accumulator name `itemSurchargeTotal` (NOT `curseSurcharge`) reflects that it sums all per-item surcharges.
22	
23	## After cycle 5
24	- Change: rewrote `itemSurcharges` from imperative `let surcharge = 0; if (…) surcharge += …` accumulator to two named expression bindings (`curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge`) summed in the return; added constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
25	- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — eliminated 3 assignments and a mutable `let`, reducing APP mass ~43 → ~26 (Δ -17). Rule 2 (reveals intent) — each surcharge component is now a named value at the call site, so future surcharges added inside `itemSurcharges` follow a clear pattern: `const xSurcharge = condition ? base * X_RATE : 0`.
26	- Naming established: pattern for new per-item surcharges is a named binding `<name>Surcharge = condition ? base * <NAME>_RATE : 0`; the return is a flat sum of these bindings (no mutable accumulator). Constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
27	
28	## After cycle 6
29	- Change: introduced loyalty-discount logic in cycle 6's green phase (`LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`, applied to `policyBase` in `priceQuoteStep`); refactored by extracting a generic `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` helper and replacing the two duplicated `step.items.reduce((sum, x) => sum + f(x), 0)` call sites with `sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium)` and `sumOver(step.items, itemSurcharges)`.
30	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the "sum f(x) over a collection" pattern was duplicated. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `priceQuoteStep` now reads as a flat sequence of named pricing terms with no `reduce` noise; the per-step total mass dropped from ~44 to ~23. The helper will be reused for sum-of-payouts in upcoming claim tests.
31	- Naming established: `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` is the canonical "sum a function over a collection" helper — use it instead of inlining `xs.reduce((s, x) => s + f(x), 0)`. `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`; loyalty discount is applied to `policyBase` only (NOT to surcharges, fee, or first-insurance), inside `priceQuoteStep`.
32	
33	## After cycle 7
34	- Change: introduced follow-up discount (`FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15`, new `isFollowUp: boolean` param to `priceQuoteStep`, `runScenario` passes `index > 0`) in cycle 7's green phase; refactored by extracting `Math.ceil(...)` at the premium return into a named helper `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` with a docstring naming the MHPCO domain rule.
35	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the spec explicitly calls out "rounded UP in MHPCO's favor" as a domain rule (visible in pending tests like "rounded UP to 198 in MHPCO's favor"); naming it pins that domain meaning at the call site instead of leaving a bare `Math.ceil`. Also opens the symmetric home for `roundDownInFavorOfOffice` that upcoming claim-payout tests require ("payout rounded DOWN in MHPCO's favor: 350.5 → 350"). Mass ~+3 — Rule 2 trumps APP, consistent with cycles 2/6.
36	- Naming established: `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` for quote premiums; the symmetric `roundDownInFavorOfOffice(amount)` (using `Math.floor`) is the expected home for claim-payout rounding. `FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15`; follow-up discount is applied to `policyBase` only (same scope as loyalty) inside `priceQuoteStep`. `isFollowUp: boolean` is computed by `runScenario` (`index > 0`) rather than inside `priceQuoteStep`, because positional context is only known at the scenario level — this asymmetry vs `yearsWithMHPCO` (customer field) is intentional and correct.
37

content.text:
All 12 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the refactoring opportunities.

## Refactor Phase Analysis

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The recent Green change added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PREMIUM`. Let me evaluate:

- **`BASE_PREMIUM`**: Currently a Record<string, number>. With `rune: 25`, does the name still fit? The test it solved is "2 runes, newcomer → premium 60 G (50 base + 5 first + 5 fee)". This test treats runes uniformly with other items — base premium × 2 = 50. The name still fits perfectly.
- **`itemBasePremium(item)`**: Still accurately describes its purpose for the rune case (returns 25). Name fits.
- **`priceQuoteStep`**: Still fits.
- **Other names**: All still match their purpose.

**Decision**: No renaming needed. The rune addition is a pure data-table extension — names remain accurate.

### Step 2: Calculate Current APP Mass

The change was purely additive (one line in `BASE_PREMIUM`). Let me look at potential refactorings.

Looking at the architecture-notes for cycle 4, I noted: "`itemBasePremium(item) -> number` (single source of base-premium lookup, **will absorb rune-block logic later**)". The next upcoming tests are:
- "3 runes (block applies) → 60 block base" — this is where rune-block logic enters
- "4 runes (no block — exactly 3 required) → 100 base"
- "7 runes → 175 base"
- "3 runes + 3 moonstones (two separate blocks) → 120 base"

The CURRENT passing test (2 runes → 50 base) is satisfied by simply per-item lookup of `rune: 25`. The block logic does NOT need to exist yet — TDD discipline forbids premature implementation.

### Step 3: Evaluate Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 12 tests pass. ✅

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: 
- `BASE_PREMIUM` table currently has `amulet`, `sword`, `rune` mixed together. The spec distinguishes "items" (amulet, sword) from "components" (rune, moonstone). Does this distinction matter at this point?
- The test for runes is in a describe-block context where it's called "components". But only one test (2 runes) currently exists, and it's satisfied by treating runes identically to other items. Introducing an "items vs components" distinction now would be premature abstraction.
- Looking at the code, intent is already clear.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: 
- No new duplication introduced by adding `rune: 25`.
- Existing code reviewed — no obvious duplication.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**:
- Adding one line to the lookup table is the minimal possible change.
- Could `BASE_PREMIUM` be inlined? No — it's used by `itemBasePremium` and is a clear domain table.

### Step 4: Looking for a Genuine Refactoring

Let me look harder at the implementation for any improvement opportunity.

Let me consider potential refactorings:

**Option A**: Look at the `QuoteItem` interface. The test data passes `material: "silver"` and `material: "steel"` but the interface doesn't declare these. Adding them would be premature (no test demands them yet).

**Option B**: Within the conditional discount expressions:
```
const loyaltyDiscount = yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS ? policyBase * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;
const followUpDiscount = isFollowUp ? policyBase * FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;
```
These have similar structure. Could extract a helper like `discount(applies, base, rate)`. But this would obscure intent — Rule 2 trumps APP. The current form makes each discount's trigger condition immediately visible. Skip.

**Option C**: The `BASE_PREMIUM` table now has 3 entries mixing items and components. Should it be split into `ITEM_BASE_PREMIUM` and `COMPONENT_BASE_PREMIUM`? Currently no behavior depends on the distinction — this is premature. The forthcoming "block of 3 runes" rule will create the distinction, but it's NOT a test yet. Skip.

**Option D**: Could I rename the `material` field handling? It's not used at all — but adding `material` to the interface without tests requiring it would be premature.

**Option E**: Look at `priceQuoteStep`'s return type `{ premium: number }`. The `runScenario` return type duplicates this shape inline (`results: { premium: number }[]`). Could extract a `QuoteResult` type alias. Let me weigh this:

```typescript
type QuoteResult = { premium: number };
```

This would:
- Eliminate the duplicated `{ premium: number }` literal (appears in `priceQuoteStep` return type and `runScenario` return type)
- Give a name to the result-shape concept ("a priced quote result")
- Rule 3 (no duplication) + Rule 2 (reveals intent) both favor this
- Upcoming tests (claim payouts) will likely produce `{ payout: number }` results too — having a named type for quote results clarifies the distinction

This is a real, minor refactoring that improves the code. Let me calculate mass:

**Before** (type declarations only — types don't count toward APP runtime mass, but are still elements):
- Two inline `{ premium: number }` literals

**After**:
- One `type QuoteResult = { premium: number };` declaration
- Two references to `QuoteResult`

APP doesn't count type declarations in runtime mass directly, but Rule 2 (reveals intent) and Rule 3 (no duplication) both favor extracting the named type.

Let me apply this refactoring.

All 12 tests pass after refactoring. The task list is just a reminder — task #8 is in_progress and not specific to this single cycle, so I'll leave it.

Now let me append to architecture-notes.md.

input.old_string:
## After cycle 7
- Change: introduced follow-up discount (`FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15`, new `isFollowUp: boolean` param to `priceQuoteStep`, `runScenario` passes `index > 0`) in cycle 7's green phase; refactored by extracting `Math.ceil(...)` at the premium return into a named helper `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` with a docstring naming the MHPCO domain rule.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the spec explicitly calls out "rounded UP in MHPCO's favor" as a domain rule (visible in pending tests like "rounded UP to 198 in MHPCO's favor"); naming it pins that domain meaning at the call site instead of leaving a bare `Math.ceil`. Also opens the symmetric home for `roundDownInFavorOfOffice` that upcoming claim-payout tests require ("payout rounded DOWN in MHPCO's favor: 350.5 → 350"). Mass ~+3 — Rule 2 trumps APP, consistent with cycles 2/6.
- Naming established: `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` for quote premiums; the symmetric `roundDownInFavorOfOffice(amount)` (using `Math.floor`) is the expected home for claim-payout rounding. `FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15`; follow-up discount is applied to `policyBase` only (same scope as loyalty) inside `priceQuoteStep`. `isFollowUp: boolean` is computed by `runScenario` (`index > 0`) rather than inside `priceQuoteStep`, because positional context is only known at the scenario level — this asymmetry vs `yearsWithMHPCO` (customer field) is intentional and correct.
input.new_string:
## After cycle 7
- Change: introduced follow-up discount (`FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15`, new `isFollowUp: boolean` param to `priceQuoteStep`, `runScenario` passes `index > 0`) in cycle 7's green phase; refactored by extracting `Math.ceil(...)` at the premium return into a named helper `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` with a docstring naming the MHPCO domain rule.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the spec explicitly calls out "rounded UP in MHPCO's favor" as a domain rule (visible in pending tests like "rounded UP to 198 in MHPCO's favor"); naming it pins that domain meaning at the call site instead of leaving a bare `Math.ceil`. Also opens the symmetric home for `roundDownInFavorOfOffice` that upcoming claim-payout tests require ("payout rounded DOWN in MHPCO's favor: 350.5 → 350"). Mass ~+3 — Rule 2 trumps APP, consistent with cycles 2/6.
- Naming established: `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` for quote premiums; the symmetric `roundDownInFavorOfOffice(amount)` (using `Math.floor`) is the expected home for claim-payout rounding. `FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15`; follow-up discount is applied to `policyBase` only (same scope as loyalty) inside `priceQuoteStep`. `isFollowUp: boolean` is computed by `runScenario` (`index > 0`) rather than inside `priceQuoteStep`, because positional context is only known at the scenario level — this asymmetry vs `yearsWithMHPCO` (customer field) is intentional and correct.

## After cycle 8
- Change: added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PREMIUM` in cycle 8's green phase (satisfies "2 runes → 50 base" via uniform per-item lookup, no block logic yet); refactored by extracting `type QuoteResult = { premium: number }` and replacing the two inline `{ premium: number }` literals in `priceQuoteStep`'s and `runScenario`'s return types.
- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the `{ premium: number }` shape was duplicated across two return-type annotations; now centralised. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — names the "priced quote result" concept; upcoming claim-payout tests will produce a parallel `ClaimResult = { payout: number }` shape, and having a sibling `QuoteResult` named type makes that distinction obvious. Did NOT split `BASE_PREMIUM` into items/components or extract a `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` constant — premature; only the 2-rune (no-block) case is currently a passing test. Rune-block logic enters in cycle 9 ("3 runes → 60 block base") and `itemBasePremium` is the established home for it (per cycle 4 notes).
- Naming established: `type QuoteResult = { premium: number }` is the canonical priced-quote-result shape — both `priceQuoteStep` and `runScenario.results[]` reference it; expect a sibling `type ClaimResult = { payout: number }` when claim-payout tests begin (cycle 13+). `BASE_PREMIUM` still mixes items and components (correct for now — distinction only matters once block-pricing exists).

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**:
- `BASE_PREMIUM`, `itemBasePremium`, `priceQuoteStep`, `runScenario` — all still accurately describe their purpose with the rune addition. The 2-rune test is satisfied by uniform per-item lookup; no semantic shift in any existing name.
- Decision: Keep all existing names.

**Refactoring Applied**: Extracted `type QuoteResult = { premium: number }` and replaced the two inline `{ premium: number }` literals in `priceQuoteStep`'s return type and `runScenario`'s return-type annotation.

**Mass Change** (runtime APP, types excluded from runtime mass): unchanged. The improvement is structural/type-level — Rule 3 (no duplication) eliminated the duplicated shape, Rule 2 (reveals intent) named the priced-quote-result concept, which also prepares a clear naming slot for the upcoming `ClaimResult = { payout: number }` sibling type that claim-payout tests will need.

**Refactorings explicitly rejected** (documented as premature):
- Splitting `BASE_PREMIUM` into `ITEM_BASE_PREMIUM` / `COMPONENT_BASE_PREMIUM` — premature; no block-pricing test passes yet.
- Adding `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` or block logic to `itemBasePremium` — TDD discipline forbids; enters in cycle 9.
- Extracting a generic `discount(applies, base, rate)` helper for the loyalty/follow-up pair — would obscure the per-discount trigger condition; Rule 2 trumps APP.
- Adding `material` to `QuoteItem` — no test demands it.

**Tests**: 12 passed, 22 todo. All green.

**Files**:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_22-55-29_claim-office-lite-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Proceeding to the next test.
